1:
"Now we beseech you, brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, and by our gathering together unto him," The witnesses note Paul's appeal by the coming of Christ and the gathering to him. On "we beseech you, brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the solemn entreaty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul introduces his correction of their error with a tender, solemn appeal: "we beseech (entreat) you, brethren, by (concerning, and by the authority of) the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the very subject of their confusion, the second coming, he makes the ground of his appeal.

On "and by our gathering together unto him," the witnesses note the great hope. Barnes and Calvin note the second part of the appeal: "our gathering together (episynagoge — the assembling, the being gathered) unto him" — the great reunion of all the saints to Christ at his coming (cf. 1 Thessalonians 4:17); the longed-for ingathering of the redeemed to their Lord.

The latin-fathers Tertullian and eastern Cyril note the linked hope: the coming and the gathering belong together — Christ's appearing and the assembling of his people to him are the one great event on which the believer's hope is fixed. The verse displays Paul's appeal by the coming of Christ and the gathering to him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's solemn appeal — "we beseech (entreat) you, by (concerning) the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ," the subject of their confusion made the ground of appeal (Barnes, Robertson); "our gathering together (the assembling) unto him," the great reunion of the saints to Christ at his coming (Barnes, Calvin); the coming and gathering one linked hope (Tertullian, Cyril). The verse displays the appeal. The verse marks the entreaty: we beseech you. The verse displays the coming: of Christ. The verse displays the gathering: unto him. The verse displays the hope: the great reunion. The appeal displays Paul entreating by the coming and the gathering. The verse displays the beseeching: solemn. The verse displays the parousia: Christ's. The verse displays the gathering: of the saints. Paul's appeal by the coming of Christ and the gathering to him displays the tender solemnity of his correction: he entreats the Thessalonians, on the very ground of the second coming and the great ingathering of the saints to Christ, not to be shaken about that day. The verse displays the appeal, against being troubled about the day of Christ (v.2).
2:
"That ye be not soon shaken in mind, or be troubled, neither by spirit, nor by word, nor by letter as from us, as that the day of Christ is at hand." The witnesses note the warning not to be shaken into thinking the day of Christ already come. On "be not soon shaken in mind, or be troubled," the grammatical voices note the alarm. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's caution against their agitation: "be not soon (quickly, hastily) shaken (saleuthenai — shaken as by a wave or earthquake, unsettled) in mind, or be troubled (thrown into alarm)" — they had been unsettled and frightened by a false notion.

On "neither by spirit, nor by word, nor by letter as from us," the witnesses note the three alleged sources. Barnes and Calvin note the three channels by which the error came, or was claimed to come: "by spirit (a pretended prophecy or revelation), nor by word (a spoken report or sermon), nor by letter as from us (a forged letter purporting to be Paul's)" — each falsely appealing to apostolic authority.

On "as that the day of Christ is at hand," the grammatical-historical note the error itself: the false claim was "that the day of Christ is at hand (enesteken — is present, has actually arrived, is now upon us)" — not merely near, but already come; this is what unsettled them, and what Paul corrects. The verse displays the warning not to be shaken into thinking the day of Christ already come.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's caution — "be not soon (hastily) shaken (unsettled) in mind, or be troubled (alarmed)," they having been frightened by a false notion (Barnes, Robertson); the three alleged sources "by spirit (false prophecy), nor by word (report), nor by letter as from us (a forged letter)," each falsely claiming apostolic authority (Barnes, Calvin); the error "that the day of Christ is at hand (is present, has arrived)," not merely near but already come (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the alarm: shaken and troubled. The verse displays the sources: spirit, word, letter. The verse displays the error: the day is present. The verse displays the correction: it is not. The warning displays Paul steadying them against the false alarm. The verse displays the shaking: forbidden. The verse displays the forgery: a letter as from us. The verse displays the day: not yet present. The warning not to be shaken into thinking the day of Christ already come displays Paul's correction of their error: they were not to be unsettled or alarmed — by any pretended prophecy, report, or forged letter claiming his name — into believing that the day of Christ had already arrived. The verse displays the false alarm corrected, by the events that must come first (v.3).
3:
"Let no man deceive you by any means: for that day shall not come, except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition;" The witnesses note the falling away and the man of sin who must come first — the great crux. On "let no man deceive you by any means," the grammatical voices note the caution. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's warning against deception: "let no man deceive you by any means" — by no trick or pretext; for the day cannot have come, since two things must precede it.

On "except there come a falling away first," the witnesses note the apostasy. Barnes and Henry note the first prerequisite: "a falling away (apostasia — the apostasy, a great religious defection) first" — a widespread departure from the faith must come before the day; Henry: not a civil but a spiritual defection from sound doctrine, worship, and holy life, which gives rise to the man of sin.

On "that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition," interpreters divide over his identity. The Reformed Calvin, Gill, the puritan Henry, and Barnes (the historicist Protestant view) hold the "man of sin... son of perdition" to be not a single individual but a kingdom or succession — the papal power, the Roman Antichrist developing through many ages. The eastern Cyril and the fathers hold him a single future personal Antichrist, preceded by the apostasy. A third reading (Warfield, noted by Robertson) takes the imperial line with its rage for deification. The verse displays the falling away and the man of sin who must come first.

The witnesses agree the structure: the day cannot have come, for two things must precede it — "a falling away (apostasia — a great religious defection) first," and the revealing of "that man of sin... the son of perdition" (the one devoted to destruction and the destroyer of many). But his identity divides them: Calvin, Gill, Henry, and Barnes (historicist Protestant) take the man of sin as the papal power, a kingdom extending through many ages, not one man; Cyril and the fathers as a single future personal Antichrist; Warfield (via Robertson) as the Roman imperial line. The verse displays the crux. The verse marks the caution: let no man deceive you. The verse displays the apostasy: falling away first. The verse displays the figure: the man of sin. The verse displays the debate: Papacy, personal Antichrist, or imperial line. The structure displays the day awaiting the apostasy and the man of sin. The verse displays the deception: warned against. The verse displays the man of sin: revealed. The verse displays the perdition: his doom. The falling away and the man of sin who must come first display the great prophetic crux: before the day of Christ must come the apostasy and the revealing of the man of sin, the son of perdition — identified as the papal power (Calvin, Gill, Henry, Barnes), a single future personal Antichrist (Cyril, the fathers), or the Roman imperial line (Warfield). The verse displays the man of sin, whose blasphemous self-exaltation is described (v.4).
4:
"Who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped; so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God." The witnesses note the man of sin's blasphemous self-exaltation — and the question of the temple. On "who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God," the grammatical voices note the self-deification. Barnes and Robertson note the character of the man of sin: he "opposeth (sets himself against, is the adversary) and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped (every object of worship, divine or revered)" — he sets himself above God and all gods, claiming a supremacy belonging to God alone.

On "so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God," interpreters divide over the temple. The Reformed Calvin, Gill, and the puritan Henry (with Barnes) take "the temple of God" figuratively as the Christian church — the man of sin (the Papacy) usurping divine authority within the church, claiming honours due to God. The eastern Cyril and the fathers take it of a literal temple (the rebuilt temple at Jerusalem) where the personal Antichrist will sit. The grammatical-historical note both readings, the church or a literal temple.

On "shewing himself that he is God," the witnesses note the climax of blasphemy. Barnes and Calvin note the crowning impiety: "shewing (proclaiming, displaying) himself that he is God" — arrogating divine honours, setting himself forth as God; the supreme self-exaltation. The verse displays the man of sin's blasphemous self-exaltation.

The witnesses agree the substance: the man of sin opposes and exalts himself above all that is called God, sits as God in the temple of God, and shews himself to be God — a blasphemous usurpation of divine honours (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But "the temple of God" divides them: Calvin, Gill, Henry, and Barnes take it figuratively of the Christian church (the Papacy usurping authority within it); Cyril and the fathers take it of a literal temple at Jerusalem where the personal Antichrist sits. The verse displays the self-exaltation and the temple crux. The verse marks the opposition: above all gods. The verse displays the seat: in the temple of God. The verse displays the debate: the church or the Jerusalem temple. The verse displays the blasphemy: shewing himself God. The self-exaltation displays the man of sin claiming the place of God. The verse displays the exalting: of self. The verse displays the temple: usurped. The verse displays the God-claim: blasphemous. The man of sin's blasphemous self-exaltation displays the height of his impiety and a crux: he sets himself above all that is called God and sits as God in the temple of God — understood as the Christian church (Calvin, Gill, Henry, Barnes) or a literal Jerusalem temple (Cyril, the fathers) — shewing himself to be God. The verse displays the self-deifying usurper, of whom Paul had forewarned them (v.5).
5:
"Remember ye not, that, when I was yet with you, I told you these things?" The brief verse notes Paul's reminder that he had taught them these things in person. The grammatical voices note the appeal to memory. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recalls his earlier oral teaching: "remember ye not, that, when I was yet with you, I told you these things?" — he had already, during his ministry at Thessalonica, instructed them about the apostasy and the man of sin; this was no new doctrine.

On "I told you these things," the witnesses note the prior instruction. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul had taught these eschatological matters in person; the brevity and allusiveness of his written words here are explained by the fuller teaching they had already received orally.

The puritan Henry notes the design: Paul had told them of the coming apostasy beforehand, that they should not be stumbled or offended when it came; forewarned, they would not be shaken. The verse displays Paul's reminder that he had taught them these things in person.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls his oral teaching — "remember ye not, that, when I was yet with you, I told you these things?", having already instructed them about the apostasy and man of sin (Barnes, Robertson); the brevity of his written words explained by the fuller oral teaching (Barnes, Calvin); the design that, forewarned, they not be stumbled (Henry). The verse displays the reminder. The verse marks the appeal: remember ye not. The verse displays the time: when I was with you. The verse displays the teaching: I told you. The verse displays the design: forewarned. The reminder displays Paul recalling his prior instruction. The verse displays the memory: appealed to. The verse displays the things: foretold. The verse displays the purpose: not to stumble. Paul's reminder that he had taught them these things in person displays the ground of his brief allusions: the doctrine of the apostasy and the man of sin was no novelty but what he had already taught the Thessalonians during his ministry among them — forewarning them, that they should not be shaken when these things came. The verse displays the prior teaching, recalled to explain the restraining power (v.6).
6:
"And now ye know what withholdeth that he might be revealed in his time." The witnesses note the restraining power that holds back the man of sin — the crux of the restrainer. On "now ye know what withholdeth," interpreters divide over the restraining power. The eastern Chrysostom holds it the Roman Empire (he "most of all accedes" to this), spoken of obscurely lest Paul provoke the empire's wrath. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical Barnes agree — the civil power of Rome, whose removal would let the papal power rise. The Reformed Calvin differs: the restrainer is the necessary spread of the gospel through all the world (the universal call of the Gentiles) before God gives loose rein to Antichrist. Gill notes and rejects two further views — the apostle Paul himself, and the grace of the Spirit's gifts.

On "what withholdeth," the grammatical voices note the neuter form. Barnes and Robertson note that the phrase is neuter here ("that which restrains, to katechon"), masculine in v.7 ("he that letteth") — a restraining principle or power, capable of being embodied in a person; something then operating to hold back the man of sin's revealing.

On "that he might be revealed in his time," the witnesses note the divine timing: the restraint holds until "his time (his appointed season)" — the man of sin will be revealed only at the moment God has set, not before. The verse displays the restraining power that holds back the man of sin.

The witnesses agree the structure: something then "withholdeth" (to katechon, neuter), restraining the man of sin so that he is revealed only "in his time," the season God has appointed; the restraint is a real, present, known power. But its identity divides them: Chrysostom, Wesley, and Barnes hold it the Roman Empire / civil power (named obscurely for fear of the empire); Calvin holds it the worldwide preaching of the gospel that must run its course first; the view that it is the Spirit's gifts, or Paul himself, is noted and rejected (Gill, Chrysostom). The verse displays the restrainer crux. The verse marks the restraint: what withholdeth. The verse displays the form: neuter, a power. The verse displays the debate: the Roman Empire or the gospel. The verse displays the timing: in his time. The restraint displays a power holding back the man of sin till God's hour. The verse displays the withholding: present. The verse displays the revealing: delayed. The verse displays the season: appointed. The restraining power that holds back the man of sin displays a famous crux: the Thessalonians knew what then withheld his revealing — identified as the Roman Empire / civil power (Chrysostom, Wesley, Barnes) or the worldwide spread of the gospel (Calvin) — a restraint that holds until the appointed time of his manifestation. The verse displays the restrainer, while the mystery of iniquity already works (v.7).
7:
"For the mystery of iniquity doth already work: only he who now letteth will let, until he be taken out of the way." The witnesses note the mystery of iniquity already working until the restrainer is removed. On "the mystery of iniquity doth already work," the grammatical voices note the present operation. Barnes and Robertson note that the evil was already secretly at work: "the mystery of iniquity (mysterion tes anomias — the secret of lawlessness, the hidden working of the apostate principle) doth already work (is operative, energizes)" — even in Paul's day the seeds of the coming apostasy were silently growing.

On "mystery of iniquity," the Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note its hidden, gradual nature. Calvin and Henry note that it is a "mystery" because the wicked designs were concealed under fair shows and pretences of devotion; the corruption (Henry: of the Romish church) came in by degrees, secretly, the usurpation gradual not all at once — already begun in the apostles' day in pride, ambition, and false teaching (the Nicolaitans, Diotrephes).

On "he who now letteth will let, until he be taken out of the way," the witnesses note the removal of the restrainer. Barnes and Ellicott note that the restraining power ("he that letteth," masculine now) will continue to restrain "until he be taken out of the way (removed from the midst)" — and Ellicott notes the prophecy is satisfied if "he that withholdeth" is a whole succession of persons, not one man. The verse displays the mystery of iniquity already working until the restrainer is removed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the evil already secretly at work — "the mystery of iniquity (the secret of lawlessness) doth already work," the seeds of the apostasy silently growing even then (Barnes, Robertson); a "mystery" because concealed under fair pretences, coming in by degrees, begun in the apostles' day (Calvin, Henry); the restrainer ("he that letteth," masculine) continuing "until he be taken out of the way," perhaps a succession of persons (Barnes, Ellicott). The verse displays the working mystery. The verse marks the working: already. The verse displays the secrecy: a mystery. The verse displays the gradualness: by degrees. The verse displays the removal: taken away. The mystery displays the lawlessness secretly working till the restraint is gone. The verse displays the iniquity: a mystery. The verse displays the letting: until removed. The verse displays the restrainer: taken away. The mystery of iniquity already working until the restrainer is removed displays the secret growth of the apostasy: the lawless principle was already at work in Paul's day, hidden under fair pretences and advancing by degrees — held in check only until the restraining power should be taken out of the way, and then the man of sin would be revealed. The verse displays the working mystery, climaxing in the Wicked one's revealing and ruin (v.8).
8:
"And then shall that Wicked be revealed, whom the Lord shall consume with the spirit of his mouth, and shall destroy with the brightness of his coming:" The witnesses note the revealing and destruction of the Wicked one by Christ's coming. On "then shall that Wicked be revealed," the grammatical voices note the manifestation. Barnes and Robertson note the sequence: when the restraint is removed, "then shall that Wicked (the lawless one, ho anomos) be revealed" — fully manifested in his open lawlessness, no longer secret but disclosed.

On "whom the Lord shall consume with the spirit of his mouth," the witnesses note the means of his ruin. Barnes and Calvin note the destruction: "whom the Lord shall consume (analoi — destroy, do away with) with the spirit (breath) of his mouth" — by the mere breath of his mouth (cf. Isaiah 11:4), the word and Spirit of Christ; Calvin and the Reformers note that the preaching of the gospel itself begins to consume Antichrist's power.

On "shall destroy with the brightness of his coming," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the final blow: "and shall destroy (katargesei — bring to nought, abolish) with the brightness (epiphaneia — the manifestation, the bright shining) of his coming" — the splendour of Christ's appearing utterly abolishes the lawless one; the second coming is his final ruin. The verse displays the revealing and destruction of the Wicked one.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sequence — when the restraint is gone, "then shall that Wicked (the lawless one) be revealed," openly manifested (Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord shall consume with the spirit (breath) of his mouth," by the breath of his mouth (Isaiah 11:4), the word and Spirit, the gospel beginning to consume his power (Barnes, Calvin); "shall destroy with the brightness (manifestation) of his coming," the splendour of Christ's appearing his final ruin (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Wicked one's ruin. The verse marks the revealing: that Wicked. The verse displays the consuming: breath of his mouth. The verse displays the destroying: brightness of his coming. The verse displays the agent: the Lord. The ruin displays the lawless one destroyed by Christ's word and coming. The verse displays the Wicked: revealed. The verse displays the breath: consuming. The verse displays the brightness: destroying. The revealing and destruction of the Wicked one by Christ's coming display the certain doom of the man of sin: once revealed in open lawlessness, he is consumed by the breath of the Lord's mouth — the word and Spirit, the gospel — and finally abolished by the brightness of Christ's appearing. The verse displays the lawless one's destruction, whose lying power deceives the perishing (vv.9-10).
9:
"Even him, whose coming is after the working of Satan with all power and signs and lying wonders," The witnesses note the man of sin's coming with satanic power and lying wonders. On "whose coming is after the working of Satan," the grammatical voices note the source of his power. Barnes and Robertson note the dark parody: "whose coming (parousia — a coming that mimics Christ's own) is after (according to) the working (energeian — the energy, operation) of Satan" — the man of sin's manifestation is empowered by Satan, an infernal counterfeit of the true Christ's coming.

On "with all power and signs and lying wonders," the witnesses note the false miracles. Barnes and Calvin note the threefold display: "all power (mighty deeds) and signs and lying wonders (pseudous — wonders of falsehood, miracles that serve and confirm a lie)" — apparent miracles, whether real wonders wrought by Satan or deceptive shows, all in the service of falsehood.

On "lying wonders," the eastern Cyril notes the deception: the wonders are "lying" both in their source and their end — they deceive, leading men away from the truth; the personal Antichrist (Cyril) or the apostate power works signs to seduce. The verse displays the man of sin's coming with satanic power and lying wonders.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dark parody — "whose coming (mimicking Christ's) is after the working (energy) of Satan," empowered by Satan, a counterfeit of Christ (Barnes, Robertson); "all power and signs and lying wonders (wonders of falsehood serving a lie)," apparent miracles in the service of falsehood (Barnes, Calvin); the wonders "lying" in source and end, deceiving men from the truth (Cyril). The verse displays the satanic coming. The verse marks the source: the working of Satan. The verse displays the parody: his coming. The verse displays the wonders: lying. The verse displays the aim: deception. The coming displays the man of sin counterfeiting Christ by Satan's power. The verse displays the Satan: at work. The verse displays the signs: false. The verse displays the wonders: lying. The man of sin's coming with satanic power and lying wonders displays the counterfeit nature of his manifestation: his coming is empowered by Satan, a dark parody of Christ's own, attended by all manner of power, signs, and wonders of falsehood — apparent miracles wrought to serve and confirm a lie. The verse displays the deceiving power, working on those who perish (v.10).
10:
"And with all deceivableness of unrighteousness in them that perish; because they received not the love of the truth, that they might be saved." The witnesses note the deception of those who perish for refusing the love of the truth. On "all deceivableness of unrighteousness in them that perish," the grammatical voices note the deceit. Barnes and Robertson note the means and the objects: "all deceivableness (every deceit, the whole art of deception) of unrighteousness in them that perish (the perishing)" — the man of sin's lies have full effect only on those who are perishing; the deception works on hearts already turned from truth.

On "because they received not the love of the truth," the witnesses note the cause of their ruin. Barnes and Calvin note the reason they are deceived: "because they received not the love of the truth" — not merely the truth, but the love of it; they had no heart for the truth, did not welcome and cherish it, and so were left open to the lie.

On "that they might be saved," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the lost end: the love of the truth was the very thing "that they might be saved" — the truth received in love would have saved them; their ruin is self-incurred, for they refused the means of their own salvation. The verse displays the deception of those who perish for refusing the love of the truth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the means and objects — "all deceivableness (every deceit) of unrighteousness in them that perish," the lies fully effective only on the perishing, hearts already turned from truth (Barnes, Robertson); "because they received not the love of the truth," having no heart for the truth, not welcoming it, left open to the lie (Barnes, Calvin); "that they might be saved," the love of the truth being the means of salvation they refused (Wesley). The verse displays the deception. The verse marks the deceit: deceivableness of unrighteousness. The verse displays the objects: them that perish. The verse displays the cause: received not the love of truth. The verse displays the loss: might be saved. The deception displays the perishing deceived for refusing the truth. The verse displays the deceit: full. The verse displays the perishing: deceived. The verse displays the truth: unloved. The deception of those who perish for refusing the love of the truth displays the justice within the judgment: the man of sin's deceit prevails only on those who perish — and they perish because they would not receive the love of the truth that would have saved them; their ruin is self-incurred. The verse displays the refused truth, answered by God's judicial sending of delusion (v.11).
11:
"And for this cause God shall send them strong delusion, that they should believe a lie:" The witnesses note God's judicial sending of strong delusion upon them. On "for this cause God shall send them strong delusion," the grammatical voices note the judicial act. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn statement: "for this cause (because they refused the love of the truth) God shall send (pempei — sends) them strong delusion (energeian planes — a working of error, a powerful deceiving influence)" — God himself, in righteous judgment, gives them over to be deceived.

On the manner of God's sending, the witnesses note that it is judicial, not the authoring of sin. Barnes and Calvin note the careful sense: God does not create the lie or tempt to evil, but, in just retribution for their willful rejection of truth, withdraws his restraining grace and gives them up to the delusion they chose — a judicial hardening (cf. Romans 1:24-28), permitting and ordering the deception as punishment.

On "that they should believe a lie," the Reformed Calvin notes the end: the delusion is sent "that they should believe a lie (the falsehood)" — having rejected the truth, they are justly left to embrace the lie; the punishment fits the sin, error for the despisers of truth. The verse displays God's judicial sending of strong delusion.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the judicial statement — "for this cause (their refusal of truth) God shall send them strong delusion (a working of error)," God in righteous judgment giving them over to be deceived (Barnes, Robertson); God not authoring the lie but, in just retribution, withdrawing grace and giving them up to the delusion they chose, a judicial hardening (Romans 1, Barnes, Calvin); "that they should believe a lie," justly left to embrace the falsehood they preferred (Calvin). The verse displays the sent delusion. The verse marks the cause: their refusal of truth. The verse displays the act: God sends delusion. The verse displays the manner: judicial. The verse displays the end: believe a lie. The judgment displays God giving the despisers of truth up to deception. The verse displays the delusion: strong. The verse displays the sending: judicial. The verse displays the lie: believed. God's judicial sending of strong delusion upon them displays the righteous judgment behind the deception: because they refused the love of the truth, God justly gives them over — withdrawing his restraining grace — to a powerful working of error, that they should believe the lie they preferred. The verse displays the judicial delusion, that the despisers of truth be condemned (v.12).
12:
"That they all might be damned who believed not the truth, but had pleasure in unrighteousness." The brief verse notes the condemnation of those who loved unrighteousness rather than truth. The grammatical voices note the final end. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of the judicial delusion: "that they all might be damned (krithosin — judged, condemned) who believed not the truth" — the end of God's giving them over is their just condemnation; they are judged for refusing to believe the truth.

On "who believed not the truth," the witnesses note the sin of unbelief. Barnes and Calvin note that their condemnation rests on a real fault: they "believed not the truth" — they had the truth set before them and refused it; their unbelief was willful, not innocent ignorance.

On "but had pleasure in unrighteousness," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the deeper root: they "had pleasure (took delight, found satisfaction) in unrighteousness" — the reason they refused the truth was that they loved sin; the will, not the intellect, was the seat of their ruin; preferring iniquity, they rejected the truth that condemned it. The verse displays the condemnation of those who loved unrighteousness rather than truth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of the delusion — "that they all might be damned (judged, condemned) who believed not the truth," their just condemnation for refusing to believe (Barnes, Robertson); their unbelief willful, the truth set before them and refused (Barnes, Calvin); "but had pleasure (delight) in unrighteousness," loving sin the root of their refusal, the will the seat of their ruin (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the condemnation. The verse marks the end: that they might be damned. The verse displays the sin: believed not the truth. The verse displays the root: pleasure in unrighteousness. The verse displays the will: loving sin. The condemnation displays the despisers of truth judged for loving sin. The verse displays the damning: just. The verse displays the unbelief: willful. The verse displays the unrighteousness: loved. The condemnation of those who loved unrighteousness rather than truth displays the righteous end of the whole judgment: they are justly condemned who refused to believe the truth — and the root of their refusal was that they took pleasure in unrighteousness, loving the sin the truth condemned. The verse displays the just condemnation, over against the election and salvation of the brethren (v.13).
13:
"But we are bound to give thanks alway to God for you, brethren beloved of the Lord, because God hath from the beginning chosen you to salvation through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth:" The witnesses note thanksgiving for their election to salvation through the Spirit and faith. On "we are bound to give thanks alway to God for you, brethren beloved of the Lord," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the turn from the perishing to the saved: "but we are bound (owe it as a debt) to give thanks alway to God for you, brethren beloved of the Lord" — over against those who perish, Paul thanks God for the Thessalonians, beloved of the Lord.

On "God hath from the beginning chosen you to salvation," the witnesses note election. Barnes and Calvin note the doctrine: "God hath from the beginning (ap' arches — from eternity, or from the first) chosen (heilato — selected) you to salvation" — their salvation rests on God's eternal choice; the Reformed press this as gracious, unconditional election to salvation, the security of the saved over against the perishing.

On "through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth," the grammatical-historical note the means: the election is unto salvation "through sanctification of the Spirit (the Spirit's setting-apart and renewing work) and belief of the truth (faith)" — God chose them to be saved by means of the Spirit's sanctifying and their believing; the end is salvation, the means the Spirit and faith. The verse displays thanksgiving for their election to salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turn — "we are bound (owe it) to give thanks alway to God for you, brethren beloved of the Lord," thanking God for the saved over against the perishing (Barnes, Robertson); "God hath from the beginning (from eternity, or the first) chosen you to salvation," their salvation resting on God's choice (the Reformed: gracious unconditional election, Barnes, Calvin); "through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth," the means being the Spirit's work and faith (grammatical-historical). The verse displays their election. The verse marks the thanks: for you. The verse displays the love: beloved of the Lord. The verse displays the choice: chosen to salvation. The verse displays the means: Spirit and faith. The thanksgiving displays Paul grateful for their election and salvation. The verse displays the choosing: from the beginning. The verse displays the salvation: its end. The verse displays the means: the Spirit and truth. Thanksgiving for their election to salvation through the Spirit and faith displays the bright contrast with the perishing: Paul owes God thanks for the Thessalonians, beloved of the Lord, whom God chose from the beginning to salvation — by means of the Spirit's sanctifying work and their belief of the truth. The verse displays their election, to which they were called by the gospel (v.14).
14:
"Whereunto he called you by our gospel, to the obtaining of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the calling by the gospel to obtain Christ's glory. The grammatical voices note the effectual call. Barnes and Robertson note that election issues in calling: "whereunto (to which salvation) he called you by our gospel" — God's eternal choice is carried out in time through the call of the gospel; the preaching of Paul was the means by which God summoned them to the salvation he had chosen for them.

On "by our gospel," the witnesses note the instrument. Barnes and Calvin note that the calling came "by our gospel (the gospel we preached)" — the gospel is the appointed means of the effectual call; election and calling are joined, the one the eternal purpose, the other its execution.

On "to the obtaining of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the goal: the call is "to the obtaining (acquiring, gaining possession) of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the end of the calling is that believers should share in Christ's own glory; they are called to a participation in his glory. The verse displays the calling by the gospel to obtain Christ's glory.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: election issues in calling — "whereunto he called you by our gospel," God's choice carried out through the gospel call, Paul's preaching the means (Barnes, Robertson); "by our gospel," the appointed instrument of the effectual call, election and calling joined (Barnes, Calvin); "to the obtaining of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ," the end being a share in Christ's own glory (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the calling. The verse marks the call: by our gospel. The verse displays the link: from election. The verse displays the means: the gospel. The verse displays the goal: Christ's glory. The calling displays election carried out through the gospel unto glory. The verse displays the calling: effectual. The verse displays the gospel: the means. The verse displays the glory: obtained. The calling by the gospel to obtain Christ's glory displays the execution of their election: God carried out his eternal choice by calling them through the gospel Paul preached — that they might obtain a share in the very glory of the Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the call to glory, grounding the charge to stand fast (v.15).
15:
"Therefore, brethren, stand fast, and hold the traditions which ye have been taught, whether by word, or our epistle." The witnesses note the charge to stand fast and hold the apostolic traditions. On "stand fast," the grammatical voices note the steadfastness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's practical conclusion ("therefore," from their election and calling): "stand fast (steko — stand firm, hold your ground)" — being chosen and called, they are to be steadfast, not shaken by the false alarms or errors that had troubled them.

On "hold the traditions which ye have been taught," the witnesses note the apostolic teaching. Barnes and Calvin note the charge: "hold (krateite — hold firmly, grasp, retain) the traditions (paradoseis — the teachings handed down, the body of apostolic instruction)" — they are to cling to the doctrines Paul delivered; "traditions" here means the apostolic teaching itself, the gospel and its truths, not later human additions.

On "whether by word, or our epistle," the grammatical-historical note the twofold channel: the traditions came "whether by word (Paul's oral teaching when present) or our epistle (his letters)" — the same apostolic doctrine, delivered both by speech and by writing, equally to be held. The verse displays the charge to stand fast and hold the apostolic traditions.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's conclusion — "stand fast (stand firm)," steadfast given their election and calling, not shaken by error (Barnes, Robertson); "hold (grasp firmly) the traditions (the apostolic teaching handed down)," clinging to the doctrines Paul delivered, not human additions (Barnes, Calvin); "whether by word or our epistle," the same doctrine by speech and writing, equally to be held (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the steadfastness: stand fast. The verse displays the holding: the traditions. The verse displays the content: apostolic teaching. The verse displays the channels: word and epistle. The charge displays believers holding firm the apostolic doctrine. The verse displays the standing: fast. The verse displays the traditions: held. The verse displays the teaching: by word and letter. The charge to stand fast and hold the apostolic traditions displays the practical issue of their election: being chosen and called, the Thessalonians are to stand firm and cling to the apostolic teaching delivered to them — whether by Paul's spoken word or his letters. The verse displays the call to steadfastness, sealed by a prayer for their comfort (vv.16-17).
16:
"Now our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God, even our Father, which hath loved us, and hath given us everlasting consolation and good hope through grace," The witnesses note the prayer to Christ and the Father who loved us and gave consolation and hope. On "our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God, even our Father," the grammatical voices note the joint address. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul prays to both: "our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God, even our Father" — Christ named first, then the Father, with (in the Greek) a singular verb following; the joint address with a singular verb a witness to the unity and equal dignity of the Son with the Father.

On "which hath loved us, and hath given us everlasting consolation," the witnesses note the gifts of grace. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of the prayer: God "hath loved us, and hath given us everlasting consolation (aionian paraklesin — eternal comfort, comfort that does not fail)" — the believer's comfort is everlasting, resting on God's unchanging love, not on shifting circumstances.

On "and good hope through grace," the puritan Henry notes the second gift: "and good hope through grace" — a well-grounded hope of glory, given not by merit but "through grace"; the consolation and the hope are alike free gifts of God's grace. The verse displays the prayer to Christ and the Father who gave consolation and hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul prays to both — "our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God, even our Father," Christ first then the Father, with a singular verb, witnessing the Son's unity with the Father (Barnes, Robertson); God "hath loved us, and hath given us everlasting consolation (eternal comfort)," resting on his unchanging love (Barnes, Calvin); "and good hope through grace," a well-grounded hope given freely by grace (Henry). The verse displays the prayer's ground. The verse marks the address: Christ and the Father. The verse displays the unity: singular verb. The verse displays the gift: everlasting consolation. The verse displays the hope: through grace. The prayer displays God's love giving eternal comfort and hope. The verse displays the love: God's. The verse displays the consolation: everlasting. The verse displays the hope: of grace. The prayer to Christ and the Father who loved us and gave consolation and hope displays the ground of Paul's intercession: he prays to the Lord Jesus Christ and God the Father — who loved us and gave, by grace, everlasting comfort and good hope — that they would comfort and stablish the Thessalonians. The verse displays the God of consolation, invoked to comfort and stablish them (v.17).
17:
"Comfort your hearts, and stablish you in every good word and work." The brief verse notes the prayer that God comfort and stablish them in every good word and work. The grammatical voices note the twofold petition. Barnes and Robertson note the content of Paul's prayer (the singular verb governing both Christ and the Father, v.16): "comfort (parakalesai — console, encourage) your hearts, and stablish (sterixai — make firm, strengthen, confirm) you" — that God would both console them inwardly and steady them firmly.

On "comfort your hearts," the witnesses note the inward consolation. Barnes and Calvin note that the first petition answers their troubled state (2:2): they had been shaken and alarmed, and Paul prays that God would calm and comfort their hearts amid the false alarms and persecutions.

On "stablish you in every good word and work," the grammatical-historical note the practical aim: God would stablish them "in every good word and work" — in all right doctrine ("word") and right conduct ("work"); the establishing is not merely inward but issues in steadfast truth and holy living. The verse displays the prayer that God comfort and stablish them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the twofold petition — "comfort (console) your hearts, and stablish (strengthen, confirm) you," God consoling them inwardly and steadying them firmly (Barnes, Robertson); the comfort answering their troubled state (2:2), calming the shaken (Barnes, Calvin); "in every good word and work," establishing them in right doctrine and right conduct (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prayer. The verse marks the comfort: your hearts. The verse displays the stablishing: make firm. The verse displays the inward: console the troubled. The verse displays the practical: word and work. The prayer displays God comforting and steadying them in truth and life. The verse displays the comfort: inward. The verse displays the stablishing: firm. The verse displays the good: word and work. The prayer that God comfort and stablish them in every good word and work displays the close of Paul's intercession: that God would console the hearts the false alarms had troubled, and confirm them firmly in all right doctrine and holy conduct. The verse displays the establishing grace, closing the chapter and opening the request for prayer (ch.3).
